node_description: This is the top node of the NOAA value model.

Through the LTEM Ready Nation goal, NOAA provides actionable environmental information that is the basis for smart policy and decision-making in a changing world. The purpose of the goal is to support the whole-of government effort to address the LTEM crisis, strengthen resilience, and promote economic growth. It builds upon a strong scientific foundation, collection of LTEM data records, and decades of engagement with interagency, academic, and private sector partners to strengthen scientific understanding of LTEM, monitor changes in the atmosphere, oceans, and land; produce LTEM assessments; develop and deliver LTEM services at global, regional, state, and local scales; and increase public knowledge of LTEM change, its impacts and solutions. Through its stewardship responsibilities and expertise, NOAA improves its capacity to monitor, analyze, and predict the impacts of a changing LTEM on weather patterns, water resources, and ocean and coastal ecosystems.

value_tree_level_3_long_name: Authoritative Observations Data, Information Stewardship, Monitoring and Reanalysis
node_description:
NOAA will maximize accessibility, transparency, reliability, usability and public trust in accordance with data community standards and by leveraging the burgeoning cloud infrastructure. NOAA’s data will also be reproducible, easily discoverable and will work with other products or systems. NOAA will embrace quality-controlled and analysis-ready artificial intelligence and machine learning data processes and decision-ready information to meet growing customer demand. NOAA will manage its non-observational information with a similar rigor and will cultivate successful relationships with private-sector solution providers.

Data stewardship is a critical factor to realize the full value of the multi-billion dollar investment NOAA makes in Earth system observations and ensure these data are available for future generations in an equitable manner. All NOAA observations supporting real-time analysis and reanalysis, forecasting and research will be treated as a vital public investment with lasting value. To achieve this, NOAA will continue to steward and curate its information holdings according to best practices among the data management community. NOAA will implement a  comprehensive archival and stewardship process that ensures all of its data and critical information  products and derived datasets are accessible, integrated, discoverable, traceable, and reusable by ensuring NOAA data collection efforts incorporate and resource long-term stewardship plans.

node_description: Assuring data quality from the point of observation to the point of delivery is critical. NOAA plays a key role in the collection, quality control, archiving, and dissemination of accurate, secure surface LTEM observations. Metadata provides descriptive, contextual information about datasets and products to make them easier to discover, use, and understand. These data are used by the agency to fulfill its mandate to describe the nation’s LTEM and detect, monitor and predict LTEM variability and change, including characterization of socio-economic impacts.

node_description: Observations at the system level, larger scales, and throughout the depths of Earth&#039;s atmosphere and ocean systems provide us data for modeling the earth’s LTEM system and the changes occurring in it. A diverse and sustained portfolio of Earth-observing systems, spanning from the ocean bottom to the top of the atmosphere, is required to effectively address the challenges and objectives in this area. Continuity of measurements is critical for ensuring the fundamental understanding underpinning international treaties remains valid in a changing LTEM.

node_description: Reanalysis datasets can be used to create a coherent, long-term record of past weather by compensating for the inherent biases of the different instruments used to take measurements at different points in the history of weather observation. Reanalysis involves a variety of data synthesis methods that are used to incorporate different datasets into one regularly spaced grid. Reanalysis datasets are created by assimilating (&amp;amp;quot;inputting&amp;amp;quot;) LTEM observations using the same LTEM model throughout the entire reanalysis period in order to reduce the effects of modeling changes on LTEM statistics. Observations are from many different sources including ships, satellites, ground stations, RAOBS, and radar.

node_description: The purpose of this MSA is to improve understanding of the LTEM system and its impacts, improving predictions and projections, and creating better informed adaptations and mitigation strategies. To achieve this, NOAA will develop interdisciplinary and integrated research that looks across Earth systems and social sciences to understand the complex, cascading and compounding impacts of LTEM change. This will require answering key questions that limit scientific understanding of the ocean&#039;s role in LTEM (such as ocean variability, ocean circulation and heat content), atmospheric composition (clouds, aerosols, and precipitation), ice sheets, global energy budget, biogeochemical cycles, and socio-economic parameters. Research to enhance LTEM adaptation will integrate information on extreme weather and LTEM across sectors and timescales with localized socioeconomic and behavioral data to assess impact and resilience scenarios, potential solutions and implementation strategies. Evidence of progress toward this objective will include more comprehensive knowledge of atmospheric chemistry and other LTEM forcing agents, LTEM modeling, quantitative short- to long-term outlooks of Arctic sea ice, projections across  global to regional spatial scales and monthly to centennial time scales, and sustaining and monitoring LTEM observing systems.

Earth System Models and analyses across NOAA help build resilience to LTEM impacts, predicting and projecting change from years to decades in the future, and improving our understanding of and ability to simulate the Earth system. NOAA’s ESMs are based on an atmospheric circulation model coupled with an oceanic circulation model, with representations of land, sea ice and iceberg dynamics. The LTEM system consists of five interacting components: the atmosphere, the hydrosphere, the cryosphere, the land surface and the biosphere. Scenarios of emissions and other drivers are used to assess the impact of a range of human activities on these components. Changes in LTEM described in LTEM scenarios are major drivers of changes in both natural and human systems. Impacts on ecosystems, natural resources, economic activities and infrastructure, and human well-being, depend not only on LTEM change, but also on other changes in the environment and the capacity of societies and economies to buffer and adapt to impacts.

node_description: Weather and LTEM attribution studies and reports are a scientific process for identifying the major causes of observed LTEM and weather patterns. Quantitative understanding of how and why specific changes are unfolding in the Earth System, and what might happen in the future, is needed for the formulation of robust policies for mitigation of, and adaptation to, LTEM change. This research includes explaining extreme events for which great public interest exists because they produce profound societal impacts and trends of decade-to-century duration.

subtree_name: kpshGrp LTEM Attribution Studies Product Group_N2-2

node_description: The ocean is the primary driver of LTEM variability on the decadal to multi-decadal timescales. Understanding the relationships and processes between ocean and atmosphere is important to describe these variations, how strongly they interact, and how well our models simulate them. NOAA’s LTEM variability and predictability research identifies the sources of predictability on these timescales, leading to future improvements in decadal LTEM prediction. NOAA’s work includes: studies of LTEM processes and mechanics; field campaigns coordinating observations and analysis; and LTEM Process Teams connecting observations, theory, and modeling.

node_description: The integration of social, behavioral, and economic sciences (collectively referred to as “social science”) into NOAA products, services, and data can improve decision-making by making information easier to understand and more relevant to the users’ decision-making needs. As an additional benefit, social science assists NOAA with: understanding and quantifying societal and economic benefits; communicating the return on investment to the general public; and prioritizing investments to better meet its mission. Social science research enables NOAA to assess the vulnerabilities to and impacts of complex LTEM and extreme weather events on community resilience. NOAA can track and predict public risk and resilience across sectors and communities, and develop relevant and actionable information for resilience and management across multiple hazards.

node_description: The purpose of this MSA is to focus on providing LTEM predictions, analyses and assessment products and services that target timescales from weeks to subseasonal and longer. Products include LTEM monitoring, development and dissemination of reanalysis products, intra-seasonal prediction, LTEM forecast outlooks, model development activities, and ocean LTEM analyses including those that improve our understanding of LTEM variability and LTEM extremes. These products will be accompanied by services that help decision makers use LTEM information, model outcomes, and understand the associated uncertainties to include LTEM adaptation and product tools and strategies.

value_tree_level_3_long_name: Environmental Predictions and Projections on LTEM Timescales
node_description: NOAA will predict hazardous coastal inundation caused by phenomena ranging from hurricanes to increased frequency of high-tide flooding due to sea-level rise. Early efforts have been made to develop and implement capabilities to provide more comprehensive coastal inundation information. NOAA has embarked on a comprehensive water initiative designed to provide better access to the specific water information people and governments need for their unique circumstances so that their plans to address water-related risks and manage water resources more effectively can be informed by the best-available science. A component of the initiative focuses on coupling weather, hydrologic, wave, and coastal hydrodynamic models to produce water-level and inundation forecasts on weather timescales from hours to several weeks.

node_description: NOAA will inform LTEM mitigation choices through atmospheric chemistry, including carbon dioxide, and LTEM system monitoring and modeling. The agency will assess the LTEM, atmospheric and oceanic implications of mitigation measures and LTEM intervention options including renewable energy, coastal and oceanic carbon removal and solar radiation management. By validating atmospheric chemistry emissions inventories, we can ensure that the information collected is accurate, reliable, and globally comparable. This is achieved by strengthening collaboration with key organizations, such as the Global LTEM Observing System (GCOS), an international program that ensures the provision of continuous, reliable, and high-quality LTEM observations.

node_description: The purpose of this Mission Service Area (MSA) is to increase the accessibility and relevance of decision support products through robust partnerships and stakeholder engagement. NOAA will deliver integrated and relevant services to inform stakeholder decisions at every level for emergency management, transportation, power generation, agriculture, coastal planning, natural resource management, recreation and more. NOAA will develop new, and improve upon existing, equitable services to get them in the hands of decision-makers to support LTEM and weather resilience — especially to combat extreme events, such as floods, fire, drought, extreme heat and coastal inundation, including in underserved and vulnerable communities, especially tribal communities. NOAA will also contribute to LTEM readiness internationally by improving early disaster warnings around the world, sharing knowledge about LTEM-proofing infrastructure and promoting lessons learned from its successful LTEM adaptation programs.

NOAA increases the accessibility and relevance of existing and new decision support products through robust partnerships and stakeholder engagement. Combining the agency’s deep expertise with partner relationships enables NOAA to deliver integrated and relevant data and services to inform stakeholder decisions at every level for emergency management, transportation, power generation, agriculture, coastal planning, natural resource management, recreation and more. NOAA gets data and information into the hands of decision-makers to support LTEM and weather resilience — especially to address extreme events, such as floods, fire, drought, extreme heat and coastal inundation. NOAA also works with international partners, collaborating on data collection, modeling, research, and sharing knowledge from its LTEM adaptation programs.

subtree_name: kpshGrp Collaboration and Partnerships Product Group_N2-2

node_description: Service delivery lies at the heart of NOAA’s mission and is critical in all that it does. Users look to NOAA for a range of data, information, tools and services, but sometimes find them difficult to efficiently and effectively access and understand. Some users seek additional support to apply NOAA’s data, information, and tools to their situation. They want answers, guidance, training, and a helping hand. NOAA seeks to understand and apply user needs to guide product and service development (e.g., subseasonal-to-seasonal predictions, LTEM services, Blue Economy, and Weather-Ready Nation). Institutionalizing and integrating service delivery processes to align with weather-, ocean-, coast-, LTEM-, and fisheries-related activities will better equip NOAA to fulfill its mission.  NOAA will adapt its own programs and functions to account for LTEM impacts.

node_description: Communities and businesses across the country need accessible, actionable LTEM services – science, data, information, tools and decision support – which are critical to ensuring that they can make informed decisions in a warming world. NOAA tools such as the LTEM Resilience Toolkit, Digital Coast, LTEM at a Glance, LTEM Vulnerability Assessment Tool, Impact-Based Decision Support Services and many others provide science-based products and services, focusing on meeting current and future user needs in a changing LTEM.   NOAA works with its U.S. governmental, state, academic, international, commercial, nonprofit and community partners to transform the “bits and bytes” it receives from around the world into timely, actionable and reliable environmental information across the entire Earth-observing system.

node_description: Healthy Oceans Long-Term Goal ensures ocean, estuarine, and related ecosystems and the species that inhabit them are vibrant and sustainable in the face of challenges. A strong understanding of these systems supports an ecosystem-based approach to management. The approach accounts for the complex connections among organisms (including humans); their physical, biotic, cultural, and economic environments; and the wide range of processes that control their dynamics. An ecosystem-based approach will assist policy makers to weigh tradeoffs between alternative courses of action. By working toward the long-term sustainability of all species, NOAA will also help ensure for present and future generations that seafood is a safe, reliable, and affordable food source; that seafood harvest and production, recreational fishing opportunities, and non-consumptive uses of living marine resources continue to support vibrant coastal communities and economies; and that species of cultural and economic value can flourish.

The primary focus of this mission service area includes the protection, restoration, and management of coastal and ocean resources. By better characterizing and monitoring the different marine ecosystem, especially arctic and benthic systems, NOAA will be able to much more effectively manage living marine resources within each ecosystem. NOAA also strives to provide documentation of its research into coastal and marine ecosystems as well as the potential impacts of LTEM change on these systems. This becomes increasingly important as LTEM changes and a growing population put more pressure on those resources. The goal is to be able to anticipate and mitigate the effects of these changes, which include ocean acidification and LTEM forcing coastal and marine biological systems, rather than respond to these effects after irreparable harm has been done.

node_description: The primary focus of this mission service area includes stewardship of the nation&#039;s living ocean resources and their habitat. NOAA Fisheries provide vital services for the nation: productive and sustainable fisheries, safe sources of seafood, the recovery and conservation of protected resources, and healthy ecosystems all backed by sound science and an ecosystem-based approach to management. Seafood harvested from U.S. federally managed fisheries is inherently sustainable as a result of the U.S. fishery management process. Using the Magnuson-Stevens Act as the guide, NOAA Fisheries work in partnership with Regional Fishery Management Councils to assess and predict the status of fish stocks, set catch limits, ensure compliance with fisheries regulations, reduce bycatch, and assess social impacts. The resilience of our marine ecosystems and coastal communities depend on healthy marine species, including protected species such as whales, sea turtles, corals, and salmon. Under the Marine Mammal Protection Act and the Endangered Species Act, NOAA Fisheries works to recover protected marine species while allowing economic and recreational opportunities.

node_description: The purpose of this mission service area is to sustain healthy coastal and marine habitats and the communities and economies that depend on them. Coastal, marine, and great lakes habitats are among the most biologically rich and economically valuable areas on Earth. Coastal and Marine habitats provide a range of benefits to our nation including playing an essential role in sustaining and maintaining commercial and recreational fisheries as well as protected species by providing shelter, feeding, spawning, and nursery grounds. Marine, coastal and great lakes habitats also provide recreational opportunities for the public, protect life and property by acting as a buffer to storms and sea level rise, support high levels of biodiversity, and maintain water quality through runoff filtration. Human activities have significantly altered coastal and marine habitats over time. Valuable habitat continues to be degraded, lost, or made inaccessible due to coastal development, land-based pollution, fishing gear impacts, invasive species, dams and other blockages that restrict access for migratory fish species and reduce in the amount and delivery of freshwater to estuaries. In addition, LTEM change and the demand for new sources of energy have the potential to cause severe and wide-ranging impacts on habitat. The degradation and loss of habitat has significant economic, social, and environmental consequences. NOAA’s mission is to ensure that essential habitats are identified and protected, all potential impacts to these habitats and their services, from human activities and LTEM change, are assessed. NOAA also provides essential advice and scientific support to assist if the management and conservation of these essential habitats.

node_description: The purpose of this mission service area is to protect and assist in the recovery of endangered and threatened species as mandated by the Endangered Species Act (ESA) and Marine Mammal Protection Act (MMPA). Through management, conservation, recovery efforts, public outreach and education, NOAA strives to ensure the recovery and survival of the protected marine species for future generations. Appropriate regulations and management strategies are necessary to mitigate the adverse effects of human activities on protected species. NOAA management issues include marine mammal and sea turtle injury and mortality as a result of commercial and non-commercial fishing, coastal development, military operations and other ocean/beach usage. Through this mission area, NOAA will improve its understanding of the status of listed and at-risk species and develop and implement robust recovery and conservation for those species listed as at-risk. Rigorous single-species and ecosystem assessments, as well as a stronger understanding of the impacts of changes on species’ status that are planned, will be a key component. Coupled with this information, NOAA will undertake efforts to effect robust listing decisions, and conduct consultations for listed and at-risk species, permitting processes, and similar regulatory efforts. Essential aspects of this mission also include developing a better understanding of marine acoustics and their importance regarding habitats and protected species.

node_description: The Healthy Ocean Science, Service and Stewardship mission provides a systematic study of the structure and behavior of the ocean, atmosphere, and related ecosystems, integration of research and analysis, observations and monitoring, and environmental modeling as they pertain to maintaining healthy ocean ecosystems and fisheries. NOAA science includes discoveries and new understanding of the oceans and atmosphere, and the application of this understanding to such issues as the causes and consequences of LTEM change, the physical dynamics of high-impact weather events, the dynamics of complex ecosystems and biodiversity, and the ability to model and predict the future states of these systems. Science provides the foundation and future promise of the service and stewardship elements of NOAA&#039;s mission. This mission, through research focused on Healthy Ocean Long-Term Goal outcomes, develops and applies holistic, integrated Earth system approaches to understand the processes that connect changes in the atmosphere, ocean, space, land surface, and cryosphere with ecosystems, organisms, and humans over different scales. Examples of these approaches include, but are not limited to, prediction and modeling the impacts of invasive species, multiple stressors on marine resources and impacts of LTEM change on arctic marine ecosystems as well as increased research and education relating to the oceans.

node_description: Resilient Coastal Communities and Economies Long-Term Goal invigorates coastal communities and economies, and lead to increased resiliency and productivity. Comprehensive planning helps protect coastal communities and resources from the impacts of hazards and land-based pollution to vulnerable ecosystems by addressing competing uses, improving water quality, and fostering integrated management for sustainable uses. Geospatial services supports communities, navigation, and economic efficiency with accurate, useful characterizations, charts and maps, assessments, tools, and methods. Coastal decision makers will have the capacity to adaptively manage coastal communities and ecosystems with the best natural and social science available.

Coastal communities in the U.S. and their economies, including tourism, recreation, and commercial fisheries, rely on healthy coastal environments. Through work and recreation, more than 70 percent of the U.S. population comes into contact with coastal waters that can contain a diverse array of chemical contaminants, excessive nutrients, pathogens, biotoxins, and marine debris, which degrade habitat quality and negatively impact human health and the services provided by ecosystems in the coastal zone. Beach advisory days due to biological contamination have more than tripled, levels of contaminants in coastal waters have risen, and marine debris has become one of the most widespread pollution problems in the world&#039;s oceans and waterways. More than 10 percent of coastal waters are considered unfit for designated uses, and more than 50 percent of the Nation&#039;s estuaries experience hypoxia (potentially lethal reduction in dissolved oxygen content). These trends are often the result of pollution runoff that can result in hypoxic events as well as harmful algal blooms. In the face of these trends, State, tribal, and Federal partners need coordinated efforts to address drivers of this degradation and reverse trends; and early warning networks to identify and predict threats to human and ecosystem health, and to implement effective and timely management efforts.

node_description: NOAA&#039;s Marine Transportation System (MTS) mission spans ports and inland waterways across U.S. coastal waters and oceans to support commerce, recreation, and national security. MTS supports the Nation’s economy, by supporting more than 77 percent by weight and 95 percent by volume of U.S. overseas trade carried by ship. By 2020, the value of domestic maritime freight is forecasted to nearly double. MTS is increasingly vulnerable to natural and human-caused disruptions that will potentially impact the viability of the economy. Increased maritime activity can stress sensitive marine and freshwater environments and increase the risk of maritime accidents. Improving the reliability and resilience of MTS will decrease risks to the economy and the environment. Through this mission, NOAA supports operational decisions for oceans and coasts with such fundamental services as marine weather forecasts, nautical charts, surveying and mapping data, real-time oceanographic information, oceanographic predictions and forecasts, and an accurate national positioning framework. NOAA brings science and search and rescue functions to bolster emergency preparedness and response to the MTS, helping to save lives and mitigate the environmental and economic impacts of hazardous incidents, including oil spills.

node_description: The Nation’s coastal zones have become densely populated areas that are utilized for a multitude of activities including recreation alongside a diverse and growing array of industries, such as commercial and recreational fishing and aquaculture, oil and natural gas production, and the production of renewable energy. While an increasing range of uses will allow coastal communities to create diverse economies, care must be taken to ensure continued access to coastal areas, sustained ecosystems, maintained cultural heritage, and limited cumulative impacts. A coastal and marine spatial planning framework is a comprehensive management approach that is designed to support sustainable uses and ensure healthy and resilient coastal and ocean ecosystems that benefit both coastal ecosystem and community economies. Combined with its capacity to collaborate with State, territory, and Federal partners, NOAA’s expertise in ocean and coastal management and planning is needed to provide leadership and support for the development of regional and place-based spatial plans, as well as the data streams to support decision making, research, and tools necessary for implementation. In some areas, NOAA and its partners collaboratively protect, support, and manage critical coastal resources, ecosystem, and their services.

value_tree_level_3_long_name: Resilience to Coastal Hazards and LTEM Change
node_description: Coastal communities contain over one half of the U.S. population, generate nearly 60 percent of U.S. economic output, and account for hundreds of millions of dollars in flood loss claims. Their vulnerability to coastal hazards increases with growing populations, declining coastal ecosystems, and changing LTEM conditions. The Nation’s coastal communities need to improve their capacity for resilience in order to absorb impacts while maintaining an acceptable level of functioning, to reduce the amount of time and resources required to return to a fully-functioning state and to adapt to future risks by learning from past disasters and adopting risk-reduction measures. Reducing vulnerabilities depends on healthy coastal economies proactively adapting to LTEM impacts through more effective land use, conservation, hazard response, and recovery planning; maintaining sustainable and ecologically sound uses of coastal resources, such as commercial and recreational fisheries and seafood production efforts; mitigating chronic stressors; and on infrastructure decisions made at the federal, regional, state, and local levels. Coastal decision makers require assessments of potential impacts based on current science-based information (ecosystem based), supported by accurate tools and technology (geospatially referenced observations), and the skills to apply them to effectively reduce the vulnerabilities of coastal communities through timely and well-informed decisions.

value_tree_level_3_long_name: Resilient Coasts Science, Services, and Stewardship Advances
node_description: The Resilient Coastal Communities and Economies Science, Service and Stewardship mission provides a systematic study of the structure and behavior of the ocean, atmosphere, and related ecosystems; integration of research and analysis; observations and monitoring; and environmental modeling to enhance coastal ecosystem management and decision making. NOAA science includes discoveries and new understanding of the oceans and atmosphere, and the application of this understanding to such issues as the causes and consequences of LTEM change, the physical dynamics of high-impact weather events, the dynamics of complex ecosystems and biodiversity, and the ability to model and predict the future states of these systems. Science provides the foundation and future promise of the service and stewardship elements of NOAA’s mission to include development of ecosystem baselines for many unique systems that make up the US coastal zone. This mission, through research focused on Resilient Coastal Communities and Economies Long-Term Goal outcomes, develops and applies holistic, integrated Earth system approaches to understand the processes that connect changes in the atmosphere, ocean, space, land surface, and cryosphere with ecosystems, organisms, and humans over different scales.

Information provided by Weather-Ready Nation enable decision makers to assess and mitigate consequences of weather, water, and LTEM-related hazards. NOAA&#039;s capacity to provide relevant information helps create a society that is more adaptive to its environment; experiences fewer disruptions, dislocation, and injuries; and that operates a more efficient economy.

value_tree_level_3_long_name: Aviation Weather and Volcanic Ash National Service Program
node_description: The purpose of this NSP is to provide aviation weather mission products and services to the domestic and international aviation communities in the terminal, en route and oceanic airspace systems. Mission services include airport warnings, Terminal Aerodrome Forecast (TAF), regional guidance including automated sources (CIP, FIP, GTG, GFA, and CVA), en route forecasts and warnings (e.g., SIGMETs and AIRMETs), aviation area forecasts, in flight advisories, volcanic ash advisories and the operations and products of the Volcanic Ash Advisory Center (VAAC), and the Center Weather Service Units (CWSU) which are embedded in each of the 21 Air Route Traffic Control Centers. Air Traffic Flow Management Decision Support Services are provided by the Aviation Weather Center (AWC), CWSU, Weather Forecast Offices (WFO), and Alaska Aviation Weather Unit (AAWU).  This mission is executed  through collaboration on provision and dissemination of aviation weather services with: The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA); The International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO_; and the World Meteorological Organization (WMO).

The purpose of this MSA is to provide tropical cyclone weather information services and products to U.S. and international users to save lives, mitigate property loss, and improve economic efficiencies by issuing watches, warnings, forecasts and analysis of hazardous tropical weather, and by increasing understanding of these hazards to understand and predict evolution, track and impacts of tropical cyclones in the Atlantic (including the Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico), the South Pacific, and the central, western, and eastern North Pacific Oceans.
The area of responsibility encompasses the region north of the equator in the Atlantic Ocean, from 110 degrees E to the eastern continental boundary in the North Pacific, and from 140 degrees E to 120 degrees W in the South Pacific.  Examples of NOAA products and services include: Tropical Cyclone Dvorak Estimates, Hurricane Local Statements (HLS), and Tropical Weather Outlooks (TWO).

node_description: The purpose of this MSA is to provide timely and accurate water resources information that spans minutes to years, including needed advances in modeling and mapping capabilities. “Information” in this context refers to water observations, data, model output, analyses, assessments, forecasts, warnings, products, and services. This information includes water quantity (including total-water level) and water quality information for surface water (overland flow, rivers, lakes, soil moisture) and shallow groundwater, spanning the domain from mountain tops to the coastal ocean. “Total-water level” is defined as water level influenced by inland freshwater flow, winds, waves and wave action, tides, storm surge, surge-on-tide, seiche, steric anomaly, relative sea-level change, sea and freshwater ice, and is inclusive of all forcings and processes (e.g. tropical, extratropical, and tsunami). Water quality is defined here to include temperature, salinity, turbidity, nutrients and other physical and biogeochemical variables. Water resources products and services support decision makers from a spectrum of service sectors including emergency management, agriculture, hydropower, reservoir management, watershed management, fisheries and living marine resources management and conservation, river commerce, municipal and industrial water supply, recreation, and human and environmental health management. Examples of NOAA products and services include: Flash Flood Guidance, Flood Watches and Warnings, Hydrologic Conditions, Forecasts, and Outlooks, Flood Inundation Maps, Soil Moisture, Snow Analysis, Precipitation Guidance, Heavy and Excessive Rainfall, and Quantitative Precipitation Estimates (QPE) and Forecast (QPF).

value_tree_level_3_long_name: Marine & Coastal Weather National Service Program
node_description: The purpose of this NSP is to provide information services and products to U.S. and international users for the protection of life and property, mitigation of environmental impacts, promotion of economic benefits, and the enhancement of quality of life with accurate and critical forecast and warning products relating to U.S. coast, coastal and offshore waters, the Great Lakes, and the open oceans. Specific services provided include marine forecasts, warnings, and advisories for the nation´s coastal waters, offshore and high seas forecasts for the open ocean, and the nearshore and open waters of the Great Lakes. Marine services also include the issuance of coastal flood products, severe storm warnings over coastal waters, storm surge services, non-routine products marine statements, ocean surface analysis, sea ice analysis and forecasts, sea surface temperature analysis, and warnings of coastal hazards (i.e., heavy surf advisories and warnings for the occurrence of rip currents).

node_description: The purpose of this MSA is to provide routine (public) products and information to support the planning needs of the public and the operations of our partners. These products and information include observations and forecasts across a wide variety of weather parameters, summaries of current and expected weather conditions, and digitally-accessible data (WFO National Digital Forecast Database) to support the ability of our partners to automate their process. Routine (public) products and services include the issuance of general weather forecasts (out to 7 days), and the many advisory, watch, and warning. Types of routine products include zone (county), state, short range (Nowcast), and recreational weather forecast products (including inland lakes and waterways). These products include forecast graphics and grids showing the movement of weather systems and fronts, the development of storms and locations of heavy precipitation.

The purpose of this National Service Program is to provide Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado short- and long-range forecasts and warnings to alert the public of possible or impending dangerous convective weather threats. Convective threats include varying sizes of hail, intense and damaging convective winds, tornadoes, and lightning that reaches the surface. These services include warnings that combine the wind and hail threats, warnings for tornadoes, and larger-scale watches for both threats, as well as national convective outlooks and other informational products.

node_description: The purpose of this MSA is to provide a systematic study of the structure and behavior of the ocean, atmosphere, and related ecosystems; integration of research and analysis; observations and monitoring; and environmental modeling. NOAA science includes discoveries and new understanding of the oceans and atmosphere, and the application of this understanding to such issues as the causes and consequences of LTEM change, the physical dynamics of high-impact weather events, the dynamics of complex ecosystems and biodiversity, and the ability to model and predict the future states of these systems. Science provides the foundation and future promise of the service and stewardship elements of NOAA&#039;s mission. This mission, through research focused on Weather-Ready Nation Long-Term Goal outcomes, develops and applies holistic, integrated Earth system approaches to understand the processes that connect changes in the atmosphere, ocean, space, land surface, and cryosphere with ecosystems, organisms, and humans over different scales. Example NOAA products and services include: Advancement of Severe Hazardous Weather Understanding, Advancing Water Cycle Science and Services for Improved Forecasting of Extreme Precipitation Events, Atmospheric Composition Chemistry, Atmospheric Models, Boundary Layer Research, Understanding of LTEM Extreme Weather Events, Monitoring Understanding Marine Weather, Processed Digital Water Level Data, Storm Scale Analysis, Predictions and Warnings Decision Support, and Understanding Great Lakes Ice and Hydrodynamic Processes.

node_description: The National Service Program is all-inclusive of the federal government&#039;s civil space weather products and services affecting a broad range of users and critical infrastructure sectors.  Key customers include the power grid; satellite-based positioning, navigation, and timing community; aviation; the satellite industry; and emergency management community.  Depending on the phenomena and corresponding impacts, a wide variety of observations are required to support these products and services across a wide range of time scales and physical domains.

node_description: The purpose of this NSP is to provide Tsunami warning guidance, mitigation, research, and international coordination. The Tsunami mission&#039;s primary operational tasks are to rapidly locate, size, and otherwise characterize tsunami sources and resultant waves, forecast coastal impacts, and assess potential hazards. Mitigation is the effort to reduce loss of life and property by lessening the impact of disasters. Education is one of the most important mitigating factors, since it encourages preparedness and awareness. Tsunami education includes production and dissemination of content which promotes public recognition and reacting appropriately to nature&#039;s warning signs, which may be the only warning of locally generated tsunamis. NOAA also promotes other mitigation efforts including outreach, preparedness, and inundation modeling studies for communities at risk. Example NOAA products and services include: Tsunami Development Models, Tsunami Information Statements, and Tsunami Watches, Warnings, Advisories, and Threat Messages.

node_description: The purpose of this MSA is to provide winter weather products and services to alert the public of possible or impending dangerous winter weather conditions which impacts public safety, transportation, and/or commerce. The primary goal of this mission is to provide users and partners enough lead time to take appropriate action, and to describe the severity, location, timing and evolution of hazardous winter weather events occurring or forecast to occur. Winter Weather mission provides products which include Winter Storm, Ice Storm Outlooks, Winter Weather, Watch, Warnings and Advisories, Wind Chill Warnings, and Ice Storm Warnings.
